ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general congee/rice porridge class. No specific snakehead fish slice porridge entry exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers rice porridge/congee broadly. No specific fish-slice porridge code exists; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for snakehead fish slice porridge or fish congee at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of snakehead fish slice gourmet porridge weighing approximately 450 g, as typically served at a Singaporean seafood soup restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Porridge (Congee with Fish) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Snakehead fish (Channa striata) raw and cooked rice porridge composite
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo provides nutrient data for Teochew fish porridge and plain fish porridge (congee) categories. Snakehead fish (Channa striata) macro profile is consistent with HPB data for lean white fish porridge. Values adapted from HPB fish porridge entry and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entry for snakehead fish (NDB 15240) and cooked white rice porridge. Per-100g values represent the mixed dish as served (fish, rice, broth, condiments). Pork broth contribution to sodium is accounted for.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Rice congee / rice porridge
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for snakehead fish slice porridge. The closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database is plain rice congee / rice porridge (GI approximately 57, medium). Fish slices and pork broth contribute negligible glycemic load. GI of 57 applied as the best defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load calculated as: (57 × 33.75 g available carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 = 19.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from pork broth and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 450 g bowl provides approximately 1440 mg sodium, which represents 60–72% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2400 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced seasoning or consume a smaller portion and avoid adding soy sauce or fish sauce at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index from refined white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice porridge base carries a medium GI of approximately 57 and delivers around 33.8 g of available carbohydrate per standard bowl, producing a glycemic load of approximately 19; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size, pair with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables, and prefer a smaller bowl to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean fish protein with low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Snakehead fish is a lean white fish with low saturated fat (approximately 0.3 g per 100 g) and provides heart-healthy omega-3 fatty acids; the primary cardiovascular concern is the sodium content from the broth, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should request low-sodium preparation and limit portion to one bowl.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Snakehead Fish Slice Gourmet Porridge is a Singaporean-Chinese dish featuring thinly sliced snakehead fish (Channa striata, also known as haruan or toman family) served in a slow-simmered white rice congee (porridge). The dish is prepared in the Teochew grandmother style, where the rice is cooked down to a smooth, silky consistency in a rich pork-based broth. Fresh fish slices are added at the end of cooking to preserve their delicate, tender texture and mild, sweet flavour. The porridge is garnished with spring onion, a dash of white pepper, and sesame oil. Snakehead fish is prized in Singapore and Southeast Asia for its lean, firm white flesh, high protein content, and perceived health benefits including wound-healing properties in traditional medicine. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, high-protein, low-fat meal providing approximately 62 kcal per 100 g, with 5.8 g protein and 7.5 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is elevated due to the pork broth base, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (approximately 57) from the white rice base requires attention from individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish or soy allergies. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast, lunch, or light dinner meal in Singapore hawker centres and seafood soup restaurants.
common_user_queries: Calories in fish slice porridge Singapore
Is fish porridge good for diabetics?
Can hypertension patients eat fish porridge?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry for snakehead fish slice gourmet porridge was found. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB fish porridge (Teochew style) category data and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy snakehead fish data (NDB 15240). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice congee data. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo fish porridge source entry or the USDA SR Legacy snakehead fish entry. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this dish composite. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo fish porridge source. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0 as snakehead fish and rice porridge contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy and HPB data. The dish contains pork broth and is therefore not halal-certified. The is_halal flag is set to false because the dish is simmered with pork broth as stated in the restaurant description. Allergen note: fish is a primary allergen; soy sauce may be used as a condiment. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice congee/porridge data; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. Glycemic load per 450 g serving = (57 × 33.75) / 100 = 19.2.
